- [TLE] Las Vegas witness search assumes verified solutions are dense enough to sample. If one trial costs `C` and succeeds with probability `p`, the expected work is `C / p`; adversarial permutations, subsets, colorings, or geometric candidates with exponentially small `p` make the exact checker correct but unusable inside the contest limit.
- [TLE] Multi-start search treats the restart count as a tuning knob instead of a probability budget. With `r` independent attempts the hit probability is only `1 - (1 - p)^r`, so rare basins require many more restarts than the visible random tests suggest; near-timeout fallback paths then dominate max-size inputs.
- [WA] Restart portfolios assume different seeds imply different basins. Deterministic preprocessing, canonical repair, stable sorting, or the same greedy scoring proxy can collapse every restart to the same state class; logging seeds and shuffling orders helps reproducibility but does not create structural diversity.
- [WA] Random tie-breaking assumes equal local scores are sampled from meaningfully different futures. If the representation deduplicates ties before sampling, samples from a sorted prefix, or maps many seeds to the same canonical representative, the apparent randomness is just a noisy way to choose the same basin.
- [TLE] Simulated annealing assumes a fast cooling schedule still crosses the relevant barriers. Theory-level convergence can require logarithmic cooling with a constant tied to the deepest local minimum, so instances whose barrier depth grows with `n` force either far too many temperature levels or a frozen walk that spends the whole budget in one basin.
- [TLE] Annealing inner loops hide an evaluation multiplier. A solver that recomputes objective, legality, or repair after every proposed move pays `temperatures * moves_per_temperature * eval_cost`, and high rejection rates still consume full scoring time even when the state barely changes.
- [WA/TLE] Reheating or annealing restarts assume temperature changes diversify the search. If the move generator, repair pass, or penalty proxy keeps returning to the same attractor, reheating only spends more evaluations around that basin; stronger perturbations may recover diversity but can make legality repair dominate time.
- [WA] Randomized candidate pruning assumes rare moves are safely replaceable by many common alternatives. Filtering out low-proxy moves before sampling, rejecting large temporary damage, or sampling only from a top-k neighborhood can assign zero probability to the one move that crosses a feasibility or score barrier.
- [TLE] Random walks over legal states assume the move graph mixes within the fixed step budget. Bottlenecks, small boundary-to-volume ratios, large diameter, or disconnected legal components make the required walk length scale with the hard instance geometry rather than with the chosen number of random moves.
- [MLE/TLE] Beam search stores the frontier as if beam width were just quality tuning. Keeping `B` full states, scores, hashes, and parent data costs `O(B * state_size)`, while expanding each level costs `O(B * branching * transition_cost)`; full-state copies or rich dedup signatures turn a safe-looking beam into max-test memory or time failure.
- [MLE/TLE] Evolutionary search undercounts population storage. A population of `P` chromosomes plus elites, offspring, fitness caches, repair metadata, and diversity buckets costs `O(P * genome_size)` memory and `O(generations * P * fitness_cost)` time before problem-specific repair is included.
- [MLE] Portfolio and multi-start solvers keep too much history for dedup, rollback, or reproducibility. Storing every candidate, random trace, checkpoint, or visited signature changes the footprint from one incumbent state to `restarts * state_size`, which can exceed memory even when each individual run is small.
- [TLE] Monte Carlo constraint sampling assumes a small sample proves a global property. Hoeffding-style bounds need `O(epsilon^-2 * log(1/delta))` samples for one bounded estimate; applying that across many constraints, states, or candidate repairs makes the required trials far larger than a heuristic fixed sample count.
- [TLE] Randomized rounding retries independent samples without budgeting the checker. Rounding `n` variables, scanning all constraints, and greedily repairing violations costs `trials * (round_cost + check_cost + repair_cost)`, and tight constraints can make almost every rounded candidate pay the full repair path.
- [TLE/MLE] Randomized backtracking with pruning assumes average branching reflects the official maximum. Dense near-feasible partial states keep many branches alive, so the randomized order merely changes which large subtree is explored first while memo tables, undo logs, and candidate vectors grow with the failed search tree.
- [RE/TLE] Recursive randomized search assumes successful paths stay shallow. A timeout cap does not cap recursion depth, undo-stack length, or per-depth candidate buffers; hard instances whose promising branches reach the maximum problem depth can overflow stack space or spend the budget unwinding failed paths.
- [TLE] Time-budget adaptive search assumes diversification actually runs on the largest tests. If the deterministic baseline is already near worst-case time, the random restart, annealing, or population phase receives too few iterations to matter, or it starts anyway and converts a borderline baseline into a timeout.
